---
title: "NLP Analysis"
author: "GROUP IV"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: false
  eval: true
---


```{python}
import subprocess, sys
for pkg in ["gdown", "plotly", "kaleido"]:
    subprocess.check_call([sys.executable, "-m", "pip", "install", "-q", pkg])

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from collections import Counter
import warnings, gdown, os
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", 60)
#print("Ready.")
```

```{python}
# ── Download & clean (self-contained) ────────────────────────────────────────
RAW = "lightcast_2024.csv"
if not os.path.exists(RAW):
    gdown.download("https://drive.google.com/uc?id=1V2GCHGt2dkFGqVBeoUFckU4IhUgk4ocQ",
                   RAW, quiet=False)

df_raw = pd.read_csv(RAW, low_memory=False)
#print(f"Raw: {df_raw.shape}")

df = df_raw.copy()

# 1. Drop internal tracking columns
drop1 = ["ID","URL","ACTIVE_URLS","ACTIVE_SOURCES_INFO","DUPLICATES",
         "LAST_UPDATED_TIMESTAMP","LAST_UPDATED_DATE",
         "NAICS2","NAICS3","NAICS4","NAICS5","NAICS6",
         "SOC_2","SOC_2_NAME","SOC_3","SOC_3_NAME","SOC_5","SOC_5_NAME"]
df.drop(columns=[c for c in drop1 if c in df.columns], inplace=True)

# 2. Drop columns >50% missing — protect salary & education columns
PROTECT = ["SALARY","SALARY_FROM","SALARY_TO","ORIGINAL_PAY_PERIOD",
           "MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE","MAX_YEARS_EXPERIENCE",
           "MIN_EDULEVELS","MIN_EDULEVELS_NAME",
           "MAX_EDULEVELS","MAX_EDULEVELS_NAME",
           "LIGHTCAST_SECTORS","LIGHTCAST_SECTORS_NAME"]
prot = [c for c in PROTECT if c in df.columns]
nonp = [c for c in df.columns if c not in prot]
keep = [c for c in nonp if df[c].isnull().mean() < 0.5] + prot
df   = df[keep]

# 3. Deduplicate on key identity columns
dup_key = [c for c in ["TITLE_RAW","COMPANY_NAME","STATE_NAME","POSTED"] if c in df.columns]
df.drop_duplicates(subset=dup_key, keep="first", inplace=True)

# 4. Compute midpoint salary
if "SALARY_FROM" in df.columns and "SALARY_TO" in df.columns:
    df["SALARY_MID"] = df[["SALARY_FROM","SALARY_TO"]].mean(axis=1)
    if "SALARY" in df.columns:
        df["SALARY_MID"] = df["SALARY_MID"].fillna(df["SALARY"])
elif "SALARY" in df.columns:
    df["SALARY_MID"] = df["SALARY"]

# 5. Fill categorical columns
cat_fill = ["NAICS_2022_6_NAME","NAICS_2022_2_NAME","SOC_2021_4_NAME",
            "REMOTE_TYPE_NAME","STATE_NAME","MIN_EDULEVELS_NAME",
            "LOT_V6_OCCUPATION_NAME","LOT_V6_SPECIALIZED_OCCUPATION_NAME",
            "EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME"]
for c in cat_fill:
    if c in df.columns:
        df[c].fillna("Unknown", inplace=True)

# 6. Date parsing
df["POSTED_DT"]    = pd.to_datetime(df["POSTED"], errors="coerce")
df["POSTED_MONTH"] = df["POSTED_DT"].dt.to_period("M").astype(str)
df["POSTED_WEEK"]  = df["POSTED_DT"].dt.to_period("W").astype(str)

#print(f"Cleaned: {df.shape}")
#print(f"Date range: {df['POSTED_DT'].min().date()} → {df['POSTED_DT'].max().date()}")
#print(f"Columns kept: {df.shape[1]}")
```


```{python}
# ── A1. Build 3-tier classifier ───────────────────────────────────────────────

# Keywords for LOT occupation label matching
AI_OCC_KW  = ["machine learning","data scien","ai ","artificial intelligence",
               "deep learning","nlp","computer vision","research scientist",
               "mlops","generative"]
ADV_OCC_KW = ["data engineer","analytics engineer","quantitative",
               "statistician","modeler","architect","computer systems"]

# Strict title keywords (unambiguous AI/ML only)
AI_TITLE_STRICT = [
    "machine learning engineer","ml engineer","data scientist",
    "ai engineer","artificial intelligence","deep learning",
    "nlp engineer","computer vision","research scientist",
    "generative ai","gen ai","llm","mlops","ml ops",
    "applied scientist","ai architect","ml architect",
]
ADV_TITLE_KW = [
    "data engineer","analytics engineer","platform engineer",
    "data architect","solutions architect","enterprise architect",
    "data modeler","quantitative analyst","statistician",
    "data infrastructure","data platform",
]

# Strict ML tool skills (only specific frameworks, not generic data skills)
AI_SKILLS_STRICT = [
    "tensorflow","pytorch","keras","scikit-learn","sklearn",
    "hugging face","langchain","llm","transformers","bert",
    "mlflow","kubeflow","sagemaker","vertex ai","azure machine learning",
    "spark ml","xgboost","lightgbm","catboost",
    "computer vision","natural language processing","reinforcement learning",
    "neural network","deep learning",
]

def classify_by_occ(occ):
    t = str(occ).lower()
    if any(k in t for k in AI_OCC_KW):  return "AI/ML Core"
    if any(k in t for k in ADV_OCC_KW): return "Advanced Analytics / Engineering"
    return "Traditional Analytics / BI"

def classify_by_title(title):
    t = str(title).lower()
    if any(k in t for k in AI_TITLE_STRICT): return "AI/ML Core"
    if any(k in t for k in ADV_TITLE_KW):   return "Advanced Analytics / Engineering"
    return "Traditional Analytics / BI"

def has_ai_skill(skills_str):
    s = str(skills_str).lower()
    return any(k in s for k in AI_SKILLS_STRICT)

# Apply classifiers
occ_col = next((c for c in ["LOT_V6_SPECIALIZED_OCCUPATION_NAME","LOT_V6_OCCUPATION_NAME"]
                if c in df.columns), None)
if occ_col:
    df["TIER_LOT"] = df[occ_col].apply(classify_by_occ)

if "TITLE_RAW" in df.columns:
    df["TIER_TITLE"] = df["TITLE_RAW"].apply(classify_by_title)

if "SKILLS_NAME" in df.columns:
    df["HAS_AI_SKILL"] = df["SKILLS_NAME"].apply(has_ai_skill)

# Combined: LOT primary → title fallback → else Traditional
def combine_tiers(row):
    lot   = row.get("TIER_LOT",   "Traditional Analytics / BI")
    title = row.get("TIER_TITLE", "Traditional Analytics / BI")
    if lot == "AI/ML Core" or title == "AI/ML Core":         return "AI/ML Core"
    if lot == "Advanced Analytics / Engineering" or \
       title == "Advanced Analytics / Engineering":          return "Advanced Analytics / Engineering"
    return "Traditional Analytics / BI"

df["AI_TIER"] = df.apply(combine_tiers, axis=1)

```

```{python}
# ── A2. Tier distribution — pie + count bar ───────────────────────────────────
COLOR_MAP = {
    "AI/ML Core":                      "#1565C0",
    "Advanced Analytics / Engineering": "#00897B",
    "Traditional Analytics / BI":       "#78909C",
}

tier_counts = df["AI_TIER"].value_counts().reset_index()
tier_counts.columns = ["Tier", "Count"]


```

***
## NLP Analysis — Job Description Insights
#This section extends the analysis beyond structured variables by applying Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques to job descriptions.
#The goal is to extract key skills, understand employer requirements, and identify hidden patterns in unstructured text data.

```{python}
import re
def clean_text(text):
    text = str(text).lower()
    text = re.sub(r'<[^>]+>', ' ', text)   
    text = re.sub(r'[^a-z\s]', ' ', text)  
    text = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', text).strip()
    return text
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer, TfidfVectorizer
text_col = next((c for c in df.columns if "desc" in c.lower()), None)

if text_col is None:
    text_data = df["TITLE_RAW"].dropna().astype(str)
else:
    text_data = df[text_col].apply(clean_text).dropna().astype(str)

text_data = text_data[text_data.str.len() > 2]
```
```{python}
vectorizer = CountVectorizer(stop_words='english', max_features=20)
text_data = text_data.dropna().astype(str)
text_data = text_data[text_data.str.len() > 2]
X = vectorizer.fit_transform(text_data)

word_counts = pd.DataFrame({
    "word": vectorizer.get_feature_names_out(),
    "count": X.toarray().sum(axis=0)
}).sort_values(by="count", ascending=False)

word_counts
```
##1. Word Frequency Table
##This table shows the most frequently used words in the job posting text. The most common words are analyst, data, consultant, SAP, architect, and senior. This suggests that the dataset is heavily focused on business analytics, enterprise systems, consulting, and technical business roles. Words such as SAP, Oracle, ERP, and enterprise also indicate that many employers are looking for candidates with enterprise software and business systems knowledge, not only general data skills.
```{python}
tfidf = TfidfVectorizer(stop_words='english', max_features=20)
X_tfidf = tfidf.fit_transform(text_data)

tfidf_scores = pd.DataFrame({
    "word": tfidf.get_feature_names_out(),
    "score": X_tfidf.toarray().mean(axis=0)
}).sort_values(by="score", ascending=False)

tfidf_scores
```
##2. TF-IDF Keyword Table
##The TF-IDF results are similar to the word frequency results, but they give more weight to words that are important across the text rather than just repeated often. Analyst and data still have the highest scores, which confirms that analytics-related roles dominate the dataset. The high scores for SAP, consultant, architect, and enterprise show that specialized business technology skills are important in this job market. Compared with simple word counts, TF-IDF gives a better sense of which terms are more meaningful for understanding job descriptions.
```{python}
skills = ["python","sql","excel","machine learning","tableau","power bi","communication"]

skill_counts = {s: text_data.str.contains(s).sum() for s in skills}

skill_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "skill": skill_counts.keys(),
    "count": skill_counts.values()
}).sort_values(by="count", ascending=False)

skill_df
```
##3. Skill Keyword Count and Bar Chart
##The skill keyword chart shows that SQL appears most often, followed by communication and Excel. This means employers are not only looking for technical ability, but also business communication and reporting skills. Python, Power BI, Tableau, and machine learning appear less frequently in this result, which may suggest that many postings are more focused on analytics, database querying, and business reporting than advanced machine learning. For students, SQL and communication should be treated as core employability skills.
```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

skill_df_sorted = skill_df.sort_values(by="count")

plt.figure()
plt.barh(skill_df_sorted["skill"], skill_df_sorted["count"])
plt.title("Top Skills in Job Descriptions")
plt.show()
```
```{python}
df_salary = df.dropna(subset=["SALARY_MID"])
df_salary["has_python"] = df_salary["TITLE_RAW"].astype(str).str.contains("python", na=False)
df_salary.groupby("has_python")["SALARY_MID"].mean().reset_index()
```
##4. Python Salary Comparison
##The salary comparison shows that jobs mentioning Python have a higher average salary than jobs that do not mention Python. The average salary for Python-related postings is about $114379.306499, compared with about $106080.000000 for non-Python postings. This suggests that Python may be associated with more technical or higher-value analytics roles. However, this result should be interpreted carefully because it shows correlation, not causation. Python may appear more often in advanced analytics or technical roles that already tend to pay more.
